title: End of module: Neural Networks II — Training

questions.text: Start with $w_1 = w_2 = b = 0$ and learning rate $\eta = 1$. Present the example $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$ with target $t = 0$. The weighted sum is $z = 0$, so the step activation gives $y = 1$ (since $z \ge 0$). Applying $b \leftarrow b + \eta(t-y)$, what is the new value of $b$?
Continuing with $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 0$, $b = -1$, present $(x_1, x_2) = (1, 0)$ with target $t = 0$. Compute $z$, then $\Delta w_1 = \eta(t-y)x_1$.
questions.explanation: $z = (0)(1) + (0)(0) + (-1) = -1 < 0$, so $y = 0$, matching $t = 0$. The error is $0$, so $\Delta w_1 = (1)(0)(1) = 0$ — the weights do not change.

questions.text: Still at $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 0$, $b = -1$, present $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 2)$ with target $t = 1$. The weighted sum is $z = -1 < 0$, so $y = 0$. Applying $w_1 \leftarrow w_1 + \eta(t-y)x_1$, what is the new value of $w_1$?

Training the data set $(0,0){\to}0$, $(1,0){\to}0$, $(2,2){\to}1$, $(3,3){\to}1$ in that order, with $\eta = 1$ from $w_1=w_2=b=0$, what is the value of $b$ at the end of epoch 1 (after all four examples have been processed once)?
questions.explanation: Tracing the whole epoch: example 1 sets $b = -1$; example 2 makes no change; example 3 sets $b = -1 + 1 = 0$; example 4 makes no change. End of epoch 1: $w_1 = 2$, $w_2 = 2$, $b = 0$.

questions.text: Starting from $w_1=w_2=b=0$ with $\eta = 1$, how many complete epochs does training take to converge (first reach weights that correctly classify every example in the set) on the data set $(0,0){\to}0$, $(1,0){\to}0$, $(2,2){\to}1$, $(3,3){\to}1$?
questions.explanation: Epoch 1 ends at $w_1=2, w_2=2, b=0$ but example $(0,0)$ has become misclassified again. Epoch 2 fixes it, ending at $w_1=1, w_2=2, b=-2$, at which point all four examples are correct — convergence after 2 epochs (running a further epoch 3 on these weights would confirm zero updates, but the weights are already final at the end of epoch 2).

questions.text: The perceptron convergence theorem guarantees that training reaches zero errors after a finite number of epochs…
choices: for any data set, given enough epochs
only if the training data is linearly separable
only if the learning rate is very small
only if there are at least three inputs
questions.explanation: The guarantee is conditional on linear separability. If no straight line (hyperplane) separates the classes, no set of weights classifies every example correctly, so training can never converge, however long it runs.

choices: a single weight update on one example
one full pass through every training example, in order
one iteration of adjusting the learning rate
the point at which training is guaranteed to converge
questions.explanation: An epoch is one complete pass over the whole training set. Training repeats epochs until a whole epoch produces no updates (converged) or a maximum epoch count is reached.

questions.text: Which of these statements about training a single perceptron on the XOR data set are true?
choices: It converges in exactly 2 epochs, the same as this module's worked example
It never converges, no matter how many epochs are run
No straight line can separate XOR's target-1 points from its target-0 points
Using a larger learning rate would make it converge
questions.explanation: XOR is not linearly separable (its 1s and 0s sit on opposite diagonals), so by the convergence theorem no weights classify it perfectly — training cycles forever regardless of the learning rate, which only changes step size, not whether a solution exists.

questions.text: What is the name for a network built by stacking perceptrons into an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer — the kind of network that can solve XOR?
questions.explanation: This is a **multi-layer perceptron (MLP)**. Its hidden layer lets it combine several straight-line boundaries, which a single perceptron cannot do.

questions.text: In the two-layer XOR network, hidden neuron $h_1$ computes OR with $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 1$, $b = -0.5$. For input $(x_1, x_2) = (1, 0)$, compute the pre-activation $z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b$.
In the same network, hidden neuron $h_2$ computes NAND with $w_1 = -1$, $w_2 = -1$, $b = 1.5$. For input $(x_1, x_2) = (1, 1)$, compute the pre-activation $z$.
Why is a single perceptron (one straight-line boundary) insufficient for XOR, while a two-layer network is sufficient?
choices: Two hidden neurons can each draw their own line (e.g. OR and NAND); the output neuron then draws a further line in terms of those two results, together carving out XOR's diagonal pattern
Adding a hidden layer removes the need for an activation function entirely
The hidden layer simply repeats the input layer's line twice for redundancy
Two layers only work if the learning rate is doubled
questions.explanation: Each hidden neuron contributes one line; the output neuron combines their outputs with a further weighted sum. That extra stage of combination is what lets the network represent boundaries — like XOR's — that no single line can express.
